Gentlemen:
Shortly, a number of Judges of our
Court will be required to file Candidates Campaign
statements Short Form Form 1:70 on the basis that
no more than $200 was received or spent during
the candidate's filing period.
One of the questions on this particular
form is the residence address and telephone number
or the individual Judge.
1, and a number of
other Judges of this Court, strenuously object
to including our residential addresses on these
publicly filed documents.

sections.question: One of the questions on this particular form is the residence address and telephone number of the individual Judge. I, and a number of other Judges of this Court, strenuously object to including our residential addresses on these publicly filed documents. The security reasons for such objections are obvious. I would like to know your comments regarding our objections and what action you would take if the information was refused or left blank.
sections.question_synthetic: Must judges filing candidate campaign statements on Form 1-70 include their residential addresses and telephone numbers despite security concerns?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provided an information sheet previously sent to county election officials and court officers on November 10, 1976, in response to the judge's concerns about residential address disclosure requirements, though the specific conclusion is not included in the available document text.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is fragmented/scanned with OCR errors. Contains both the incoming request (Judge Lui's October 20, 1976 letter) and FPPC's response (November 12, 1976). The actual conclusion/advice is referenced as an attached information sheet not fully visible in the document. Confidence reduced due to poor document quality and missing attachment details.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: One of the questions on this particular form is the residence address and telephone number of the individual Judge. I, and a number of other Judges of this Court, strenuously object to including our residential addresses on these publicly filed documents. The security reasons for such objections are obvious. I would like to know your comments regarding our objections and what action you would take if the information was refused or left blank.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provided an information sheet previously sent to county election officials and court officers on November 10, 1976, in response to the judge's concerns about residential address disclosure requirements, though the specific conclusion is not included in the available document text.

embedding.summary: Judge Elwood Lui requested FPPC guidance on whether judges must disclose residential addresses and phone numbers on publicly filed candidate campaign statements (Form 1-70), citing security concerns. The FPPC responded by referencing an information sheet distributed to election officials addressing such disclosure requirements.
